perturbation.original_text: The Stilwell Group agrees to comply with the Company's insider trading and disclosure policies, as in effect from time to time, to the same extent as if it were a director of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Stilwell Group agrees to comply with the Company's insider trading and disclosure policies, as in effect from time to time, but only when convenient for their business operations.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the text to allow the Stilwell Group to comply with insider trading policies only when convenient contradicts the strict liability and requirements for compliance with insider trading laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 prohibits the use of any manipulative or deceptive device or contrivance in contravention of rules and regulations prescribed by the SEC. Rule 10b-5 makes it illegal to engage in any act that would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. Allowing compliance only when convenient directly opposes these prohibitions.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the legal obligation to comply with insider trading and disclosure policies. The original text mandates compliance 'to the same extent as if it were a director of the Company.' The changed text adds 'but only when convenient for their business operations,' which introduces a condition that undermines the strict legal requirements of insider trading laws. Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as mentioned in the Law Explanation, prohibits manipulative or deceptive devices, and Rule 10b-5 makes it illegal to engage in any act that would operate as a fraud or deceit. Compliance only when 'convenient' creates an exception to these rules, directly opposing their intent. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'Manipulative and deceptive devices' under 15 USC 78j, which confirms the illegality of actions that could be construed as insider trading. The modification introduces an 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' as defined in the instructions.

perturbation.original_text: 6. This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Washington, without regard to choice of law principles that may otherwise compel the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction. Each of the parties hereby irrevocably consents to the exclusive jurisdiction of the state and federal courts sitting in the State of Washington to resolve any dispute arising from this Agreement and waives any defense of inconvenient or improper forum.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Washington, without regard to choice of law principles that may otherwise compel the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction. Each of the parties hereby consents to the jurisdiction of any court of their choosing to resolve any dispute arising from this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specification of 'exclusive jurisdiction of the state and federal courts sitting in the State of Washington' and allowing any court of their choosing, the agreement now contradicts standard contract law principles regarding forum selection clauses and could lead to disputes over jurisdiction, which are generally resolved by the agreed-upon forum selection clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1404(a) allows for a change of venue "[f]or the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought." The original clause, agreeing to exclusive jurisdiction, is standard. Removing exclusivity throws that out of the window.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., §§ 119 , 163 (Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 58, 36 Stat. 1103 ; Sept. 8, 1916, ch. 475, § 5, 39 Stat. 851 ). Section co
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'exclusive jurisdiction of the state and federal courts sitting in the State of Washington,' while the changed text allows jurisdiction in 'any court of their choosing.' This removes the initially agreed-upon exclusive jurisdiction. While 28 U.S.C. § 1404(a), as shown in both scraped snippets, allows for a change of venue for convenience, the original contract establishes a specific venue. Removing the exclusivity undermines this agreement and creates a contradiction because it opens the door for disputes outside the agreed-upon jurisdiction, effectively nullifying the original forum selection clause.

perturbation.original_text: 4. The Stilwell Group acknowledges that with regard to its obligations to maintain the confidentiality of nonpublic information of the Company and its subsidiaries, monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement and that, in addition to all other remedies, the Company may be entitled to seek specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for such breach, and in conjunction therewith the Company shall not be required to post any bond.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The Stilwell Group acknowledges that with regard to its obligations to maintain the confidentiality of nonpublic information of the Company and its subsidiaries, monetary damages will be the sole and exclusive remedy for any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement. The Company shall be required to post a bond equal to 10 times the value of the alleged damages before seeking any legal remedies.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the remedy to monetary damages and requiring an exorbitant bond contradicts the principle that injunctive relief is often necessary to protect confidential information, potentially violating trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 19.108.020 of the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act (WUTSA) allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. The modified text prohibits injunctive relief, directly contradicting WUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.020: Remedies for misappropriation—Injunction, royalty. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.020 19.108.010 << 19.108.020 >> 19.108.030 PDF RCW 19.108.020 Remedies for misappropriation — Injunction, royalty. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. [ 1981 c 286 s 2 .]
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts RCW 19.108.020, which allows for injunctive relief in cases of trade secret misappropriation. The modified contract limits the remedy to monetary damages and requires a bond, effectively prohibiting injunctive relief. This is a direct contradiction of the law, as the statute explicitly states that "Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined."

perturbation.original_text: 1. In connection with Schneider serving on the Company's board, Schneider and other Company employees, directors, and agents may divulge nonpublic information concerning the Company and its subsidiaries to the Stilwell Group and such information may be shared among the Stilwell Group's employees and agents who have a need to know such information. The Stilwell Group expressly agrees to maintain all nonpublic information concerning the Company and its subsidiaries in confidence.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. In connection with Schneider serving on the Company's board, Schneider and other Company employees, directors, and agents may divulge nonpublic information concerning the Company and its subsidiaries to the Stilwell Group and such information may be shared among the Stilwell Group's employees and agents. The Stilwell Group is not obligated to maintain the nonpublic information concerning the Company and its subsidiaries in confidence if they believe such disclosure is in their best financial interest.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to maintain confidentiality and allowing disclosure based on the Stilwell Group's best financial interest directly contradicts insider trading laws and fiduciary duties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 prohibits employing any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, making any untrue statement of a material fact, or omitting to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or engaging in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. Allowing the Stillwell group to do what is financially best for them directly is in contradiction to this law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, which prohibits employing any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By removing the obligation to maintain confidentiality and allowing the Stilwell Group to disclose nonpublic information if it's in their best financial interest, the changed text enables actions that could operate as a fraud or deceit upon others. This directly violates subsection (c) of the law snippet, which prohibits engaging in 'any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security.'
